text: Interesting work! Are you developing this query environment to be used by mostly non-coding people? Did you designed any interesting/unconventional ways to set up the documentation of your building blocks?

elements.text: Interesting work! Are you developing this query environment to be used by mostly non-coding people? Did you designed any interesting/unconventional ways to set up the documentation of your building blocks?

text: Yes, the idea is to make the data accessible to our non-coding biologists. It also is designed to help people become coders, so it displays the generated text Datalog query (in Clojure/Datomic form, and also in a R Datalog dialect called Wick used locally).

text: As for documentation, the blocks link out to a separate system I built for documenting the schema (there’s a partial picture in the deck). Don’t know how unconventional it is; it draws a schema diagram with links to pages for the individual classes.

text:
Sure, I started doing the survey. However, the survey progress doesn’t take into account answers to previous questions.

For example, I said “no” to “do you listen to something while commuting”, but the next question still asked me “what do you listen to”.

Also, at some point I had to add the option “Car CD player” to “what app do you use to listen to music” :grin: Because that’s true.

I’d say the survey assumes a lot about people’s way of life.

Also, at some point I had to add the option “Car CD player” to “what app do you use to listen to music”
Because that’s true.
I’d say the survey assumes a lot about people’s way of life.

text:
"Illustrator, Google Search, Twitter, Slack, Google Docs" are mentioned as examples that aren't public goods. I wouldn't call any of them 'transformative' (considering the only example OP explicitly gives of the term is Hindu-Arabic numerals, which feels on a different level).

Also, the phrase "programmer tools" in that quote is very odd indeed.

text:
Here are some recent transformative tools for thought on the level of Hindu-Arabic numerals, IMO:

* Typesetting (spaces between words which are arguably as important as the invention of zero and place values; punctuation marks; folios; other advances gained from the publishing industry since Gutenberg)
* Double-entry book-keeping.
* Tags (the idea of giving things multiple labels rather than forcing them to go into a single pigeonhole. Laborious using cross-indexing in the pre-computer era; really only took off in the post-Gmail, post-Delicious era)
* Hyperlinks. Arguably just a consequence of tags becoming _really_ cheap.

text:
Brainstorming Agaram-scale tools for thought is fun..
• LSD, hot showers, hammock time, and other means of shaking the tree of one's subconscious
• parametric modelling, CAD, and in general 3d graphics allow a very different relationship to space and form than was possible via sculpture and drawing, in ways I can personally attest to but can't quite put my finger on (ability to scale, perhaps?)
• sequential imagery, especially in comics and film, as a way of conveying narrative without linguistics
• games as sophisticated systems, which allow for the conveyance of all sorts of otherwise indescribable ideas
• telescopy and microscopy, history and futurism — together, these created a general awareness of the narrow scope of one's experience in life, allowing for thinking on the scale of Long Now (etc)

text: <@UCUSW7WVD> I'm curious about the part where you said "Illustrator, Google Search, Twitter, Slack, Google Docs" aren't "public goods". I'm assuming you mean that because they're closed-source software owned by big corporations, but I'd love to hear more about how that relates to whether they are "transformative tools"? Does being a closed-source piece of software prevent it from being a transformative tool?

text:
While those aren't the tools I'd personally have listed, I'll try to put on the author's shoes for a minutes and give an explanation for the transformative qualities of the listed software:

* *Illustrator*: First time graphics could be drawn digitally (rather than programmed).
* *Google Search*: Fastest access to information.
* *Twitter*: Large-scale access for an individual to broadcast out information and for other interested people to be able to find them.
* *Slack*: Real-time communication with inline files, combined with features for asynchronous participation, notably extensive history.
* *Google Docs*: Easiest way to collaborate on a document.

Anyway, again not what I would have picked, but hopefully I got close to what the authors are going for.

text:
<@UE0ETTCG7> I'll just quote directly from OP the only place that constructs the precise list you mentioned:

> We already discussed some examples where companies have partially or totally avoided the public goods problem, tools such as: Illustrator, Google Search, Twitter, Slack, Google Docs, programmer tools, and so on.
So the list is of tools of thought _that are owned by someone_.

I certainly agree with you about the value that each of these tools bring. All I'm saying is, if Slack and Twitter are in the top-10 list of transformative tools of thought, the human race is in far worse shape than even I think.

text: Yeah, I don’t really follow or agree with the public goods argument in the article either, the authors are using Adobe's product as an example of products that become public goods, and therefore are easy to replicate and aren’t beneficial for businesses to create. But Adobe is one of the most successful tech companies in the world, and have successfully created one of the most effective moats around their products ever. So I think the authors are mistaken on that point. They’re using one data point, Sketch and Figma, which has an alternative explanation I wrote about here <https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=22148158|https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=22148158> Outside of UI design, in video editing, illustration, print design, motion graphics, and photography Adobe's products are all the most popular in their category by a wide margin.

text: <@UCUSW7WVD> The authors don’t have a negative opinion of them not being public goods, actually they’re saying the reverse, that them being public goods (not the products themselves, but their implementation details) is why less tools like this are developed. I disagree with that point, but it sounded were saying they actually *aren’t public goods*, and that transformative tools for thought should or need to be, so I was curious to here more about that

text: I don't mean to add a negative connotation either. All I'm saying is: just like in an SQL query, adding a qualifier shrinks the size of the list. The U19 champion is often not as good at any sport as the world champion.

text:
<@UC2A2ARPT> (It's Roben not Robert but np) Some examples of relatively new transformative tools for thought are Trello, ObservableHQ <https://observablehq.com|https://observablehq.com> (I think this space hasn't really been nailed yet, this is where I want to go with <https://repla.app|https://repla.app>), Figma is interesting as adding collaboration to UI design, but I think Figma's success is more a byproduct of flat UI design than anything else (I don't see them surviving if things skewed back towards skewmorphism for example), Procreate <https://procreate.art|https://procreate.art> definitely qualifies.

Personally I think the most promising *direction* is creating a canonically way to share and edit multimedia documents. Today HTML is clearly the best way, but you either have to be an expert to use it, or use a specialized tool that comes with a whole bunch of baggage. There’s nothing to create and edit a multimedia document today that feels as pure as editing a bitmap with Photoshop

attachments.title: Procreate® - Made for Artists
attachments.text: Procreate is the most powerful and intuitive digital illustration app available for iPad. It's packed with features artists love, and it's an iPad exclusive.

text:
I'm still reading OP, and it's super great. It seems to have 4 distinct segments, and I'm not sure why they aren't separate articles:

* Description of a new tool using spaced repetition.
* Spaced repetition as a new domain for tools. Its benefits and limitations.
* Some examples of recent tools of thought.
* Why industry doesn't invest more in tools of thought.

There's at least one seam here.

* Why industry doesn't invest more in tools of thought.

There's at least one seam here.

text:
Unfortunately, type form only let's you do a "logic jump" if you are using the paid version. That is why he could not bring people to the end as soon as they answered "No".

About the Car CD Player, i guess you have a text option if you choose "Other" in that question.

text:
Macromedia Director had a programming language called Lingo, which was a close cousin of HyperTalk in HyperCard. And indeed, there are similarities between them and JavaScript/ActionScript, for example:

“The Hypercard event model inspired the pattern for adding events to the HTML document.”
<https://www.computer.org/csdl/magazine/co/2012/02/mco2012020007/13rRUy08MzA>

text: Welcome <@UR0AX2VHB>! Just discovered the Behave language mentionned in the end of the presentation. I like it! :slightly_smiling_face: Very simple and expressive, and it looks like it is also easy to program with it with a nicely guided drag'n drop editing (governed by types). It is what I found on this page: <http://alumni.media.mit.edu/~mt/behave/behave.html> Any other material about it? Download link? Sources?

elements.text: Welcome
! Just discovered the Behave language mentionned in the end of the presentation. I like it!
Very simple and expressive, and it looks like it is also easy to program with it with a nicely guided drag'n drop editing (governed by types). It is what I found on this page:
Any other material about it? Download link? Sources?

text: Thanks! That page is about the only thing I ever put out about it, unfortunately (never was good about publicizing my work). The code is in Mac Common Lisp and depends on a 25-year-old (pre-unix) version of MacOS, so not clear how useful it would be…but I’ll see if I can dredge it up somewhere.

text: Bidirectional links might be the most compelling tool for thought that I've seen recently, and graph databases by extension (all objects and data are connected bidirectionally). This is from my experience using Roam Research.

text: The tool for thought that I'd most like to see (and am working on somewhat) is a more interactive and visual way to edit the AST of a program. I'm curious what ways there are to turn 1-dimensional code (a sequence of characters/tokens) into something 2-dimensional, ideally that encodes a sense of compositionality

text: Something I find interesting, is the "a [shark]" block followed by the "it" block in the screenshot below. It makes the code very readable and intuitive.

elements.text: Something I find interesting, is the "a [shark]" block followed by the "it" block in the screenshot below. It makes the code very readable and intuitive.

text: It makes code very expressive. Without it, one would have used a variable, which introduces uneccessary verbosity.

elements.text: It makes code very expressive. Without it, one would have used a variable, which introduces uneccessary verbosity.

text:
But I wonder how it scales. For simple cases like this one it is ok. But il there are several ones (i.e. several "a [...]" boxes) it can be challenging to offer the good semantic. Maybe by introducing a "the [...]" bock? Like that for example (sorry for removing te beautiful graphics and colors!! :wink: ):
```when [
    nearer than [ 13 ]
    to [ a [ shark ] ]
]
then [
    if [
        a [ shelter ]
        is [ nearer than [ 30 ] ]
    ]
    then [
        go to [ the [ shelter ] ]
    ]
    else [
        go away from [ the [ shark ] ]
        by [ 30 ]
    ]
]```

elements.text: But I wonder how it scales. For simple cases like this one it is ok. But il there are several ones (i.e. several "a [...]" boxes) it can be challenging to offer the good semantic. when [
    nearer than [ 13 ]
    to [ a [ shark ] ]
]
then [
    if [
        a [ shelter ]
        is [ nearer than [ 30 ] ]
    ]
    then [
        go to [ the [ shelter ] ]
    ]
    else [
        go away from [ the [ shark ] ]
        by [ 30 ]
    ]
]

text:
FWIW, AppleScript has an "it" keyword: <https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/AppleScript/Conceptual/AppleScriptLangGuide/conceptual/ASLR_fundamentals.html#//apple_ref/doc/uid/TP40000983-CH218-SW4>

You can see a table of all AppleScript keywords here: <https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/AppleScript/Conceptual/AppleScriptLangGuide/reference/ASLR_keywords.html#//apple_ref/doc/uid/TP40000983-CH222-SW2>

text:
Yeah there is a certain art to getting the types, visual elements, and words to be aligned in a way that makes things clear.

As for `it`, I probably got the idea from Paul Graham who wrote about anaphoric macros in his book _On Lisp_. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anaphoric_macro>

attachments.title: Anaphoric macro
attachments.text: An anaphoric macro is a type of programming macro that deliberately captures some form supplied to the macro which may be referred to by an anaphor (an expression referring to another). Anaphoric macros first appeared in Paul Graham's On Lisp and their name is a reference to linguistic anaphora—the use of words as a substitute for preceding words.

text:
Oh wow, hi!

I spent a good chunk of time in I think 2016 learning everything I could about the Vivarium project - it was very direct inspiration for a project I did with a friend at the Communications Design Group. It was using the system that preceded <http://www.dynamicland.org|www.dynamicland.org>

I'd love to see more of your current work!

elements.text:

I spent a good chunk of time in I think 2016 learning everything I could about the Vivarium project - it was very direct inspiration for a project I did with a friend at the Communications Design Group. It was using the system that preceded
www.dynamicland.org
I'd love to see more of your current work!

text:
Cool! There’s not a lot published on Vivarium, but I guess Dynamicland and friends has incorporated a good bit of its genome…
The stuff I’ve done since MIT hasn’t been particularly Vivariumlike, but some of it is collected on my personal site: <http://hyperphor.com/>
